options.text: increases
decreases

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Activation energy of a chemical reaction can be determined by ________.
question_html: Activation energy of a chemical reaction can be determined by ________.

options.text: determining the rate constant at standard temperature.
options.html: determining the rate constant at standard temperature.

options.text: determining the rate constants at two temperatures.
options.html: determining the rate constants at two temperatures.
options.explanation: {tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left(\frac{1}{T_{1}}-\frac{1}{T_{2}}\right){/tex} where, Ea = activation energy T 2 = higher temperature T 1 = lower temperature k 1 = value of the rate constant at temperature T 1 k 2 = value of the rate constant at temperature T 2 This equation is known as the Arrhenius equation.

options.text: determining the probability of collision.
using catalyst.

correct_option_text: determining the rate constants at two temperatures.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Consider Figure and mark the correct option.
question_html: Consider Figure and mark the correct option.

options.text: Activation energy of forward reaction is E 1 + E 2 and product is less stable than reactant.
options.html: Activation energy of forward reaction is E1 + E2 and product is less stable than reactant.
options.explanation: E a (forward) E 1 + E 2 Since the energy of reactants is less than products then the product is less stable than the reactant.

options.text: The activation energy of the forward reaction is E 1 + E 2 and the product is more stable than reactant.
options.html: The activation energy of the forward reaction is E1 + E2 and the product is more stable than reactant.

options.text: The activation energy of both forward and backward reaction is E 1 + E 2 and the reactant is more stable than the product.
options.html: The activation energy of both forward and backward reaction is E1 + E2 and the reactant is more stable than the product.

options.text: The activation energy of the backward reaction is E 1 and the product is more stable than reactant.
options.html: The activation energy of the backward reaction is E1 and the product is more stable than reactant.

correct_option_text: Activation energy of forward reaction is E 1 + E 2 and product is less stable than reactant.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Consider a first order gas phase decomposition reaction given below: A(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B(g) + C(g) The initial pressure of the system before decomposition of A was p i . After the lapse of time t , total pressure of the system increased by x units and became p t . The rate constant k for the reaction is given as ________.
question_html:
Consider a first order gas phase decomposition reaction given below:
A(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B(g) + C(g)
The initial pressure of the system before decomposition of A was pi. After the lapse of time t, total pressure of the system increased by x units and became pt. The rate constant k for the reaction is given as ________.

options.text: {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{p_{i}}{p_{i}-x}{/tex}
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{p_{t}}{2 p_{i}-p_{t}}{/tex}
options.explanation: Let p A , p B, and pc are the partial pressures of A, B and C respectively. If x atm be the decrease in the partial pressure of A at time t and one mole each of B and C is being formed, the increase in the partial pressure of B and C will also x atm each. A(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B(g) + C(g) At t = 0 p i atm 0 atm 0 atm At time t (p i - x) x atm x atm x atm Where p i is the initial pressure at time t = 0 pt = (p i - x) atm + x + x = p i + x or x = (p t - p i ) Where p A = p i - x on substituting the value of x p A = p i - (p t - p i ) = 2p i - p t According to the first order reaction rate constant {tex}k = \frac {2.303}{t \ log} \frac {p_i}{2p_i - p_t}{/tex}

options.text: {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{p_{t}}{2 p_{t}+p_{t}}{/tex}
{tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{p_{t}}{p_{i}+x}{/tex}

correct_option_text: {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{p_{t}}{2 p_{i}-p_{t}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: According to the Arrhenius equation rate constant k is equal to Ae -E a /RT . Which of the following options represents the graph of ln k vs {tex}\frac{1}{T}{/tex} ?
question_html: According to the Arrhenius equation rate constant k is equal to Ae-Ea/RT. Which of the following options represents the graph of ln k vs {tex}\frac{1}{T}{/tex}?

options.explanation: According to Arrhenius equation k = Ae -E a /RT Taking log on both sides in k {tex}=\ln \left(\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{\mathrm{E}_{2}}{\mathrm{RT}}}\right){/tex} ln k {tex}=\ln \mathrm{A}-\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}{/tex} {tex}\ln k=-\frac{-E_{a}}{R} \times \frac{1}{T}+\ln A{/tex} y = mx + c This equation can be related to the equation of a straight line. From the graph, it is very clearly shown that the slope of the plot {tex}=\frac{-E_{a}}{R}{/tex} and intercept = ln A.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Consider the Arrhenius equation given below and mark the correct option. K = Ae -E a /RT
question_html:
Consider the Arrhenius equation given below and mark the correct option.
K = Ae-Ea/RT

options.text: Rate constant increases exponentially with increasing activation energy and decreasing temperature.
options.html: Rate constant increases exponentially with increasing activation energy and decreasing temperature.

options.text: Rate constant decreases exponentially with increasing activation energy and decreasing temperature.
options.html: Rate constant decreases exponentially with increasing activation energy and decreasing temperature.

options.text: Rate constant increases exponentially with decreasing activation energy and decreasing temperature.
options.html: Rate constant increases exponentially with decreasing activation energy and decreasing temperature.

options.text: Rate constant increases exponentially with decreasing activation energy and increasing temperature.
options.html: Rate constant increases exponentially with decreasing activation energy and increasing temperature.
options.explanation: K = Ae -E a /RT from the equation it is clear that the value of rate constant k increases exponentially with a decrease in activation energy E a and increase in temperature. As E a decreases {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}{/tex} increases and k (rate constant) increases. As T increases, {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}{/tex} decreases and {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}{/tex} increases and k (rate constant) increases.

correct_option_text: Rate constant increases exponentially with decreasing activation energy and increasing temperature.

question_html: A graph of volume of hydrogen released vs time for the reaction between zinc and dil.HCl is given in Figure. On the basis of this mark the correct option.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following statements is not correct about order of a reaction.
question_html: Which of the following statements is not correct about order of a reaction.

options.text: The order of a reaction can be a fractional number.
options.html: The order of a reaction can be a fractional number.

options.text: The order of a reaction is experimentally determined quantity.
options.html: The order of a reaction is experimentally determined quantity.

options.text: The order of a reaction is always equal to the sum of the stoichiometric coefficients of reactants in the balanced chemical equation for a reaction.
options.html: The order of a reaction is always equal to the sum of the stoichiometric coefficients of reactants in the balanced chemical equation for a reaction.
options.explanation: Order of reaction is equal to the sum of powers of concentration of the reactants in rate law expression. For any chemical reaction x A + y B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Product Rate = k[A] x [B] y Order n = x + y The order of reaction can be a fraction also. The order of a reaction is not always equal to the sum of the stoichiometric coefficients of reactants in the balanced chemical equation. For a reaction, it may or may not be equal to the sum of the stoichiometric coefficient of reactants.

options.text: The order of a reaction is the sum of the powers of the molar concentration of the reactants in the rate law expression.
options.html: The order of a reaction is the sum of the powers of the molar concentration of the reactants in the rate law expression.

correct_option_text: The order of a reaction is always equal to the sum of the stoichiometric coefficients of reactants in the balanced chemical equation for a reaction.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following statements is correct?
question_html: Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: The rate of a reaction decreases with passage of time as the concentration of reactants dereases.
options.html: The rate of a reaction decreases with passage of time as the concentration of reactants dereases.
options.explanation: Explanation: The rate of a reaction can be defined as the change in concentration of a reactant or product in unit time. To be more specific, it can be expressed in term of The rate of decrease in the concentration of any one of the reactants, or The rate of increase in the concentration of any one of the products. The concentration of reactant decreases with the passage of time, we can say that the rate of reaction is also decreasing, {tex}r = \frac {-dx}{dt}(r=rate){/tex}

options.text: The rate of a reaction is same at any time during the reaction.
options.html: The rate of a reaction is same at any time during the reaction.

options.text: The rate of a reaction is independent of temperature change.
options.html: The rate of a reaction is independent of temperature change.

options.text: The rate of a reaction decreases with increase in concentration of reactant(s).
options.html: The rate of a reaction decreases with increase in concentration of reactant(s).

correct_option_text: The rate of a reaction decreases with passage of time as the concentration of reactants dereases.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following expressions is correct for the rate of reaction given below? 5Br – (aq) + BrO – 3 (aq) + 6H + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3Br 2 (aq) + 3H 2 O(l)
question_html:
Which of the following expressions is correct for the rate of reaction given below?
5Br–(aq) + BrO–3(aq) + 6H+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3Br2(aq) + 3H2O(l)

options.text: {tex}\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} =5 \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{*}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} {/tex}
{tex}\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} =\frac{6}{5} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{\prime}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} =\frac{5}{6} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
options.explanation: For the equation 5Br – (aq) + BrO – 3(aq) + 6H + (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3Br 2(aq) + 3H 2 O (l) Rate law expression can be written as {tex}\quad \frac{-1}{5} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{BrO}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{-1}{6} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{+1}{3} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br} \dot{\mathrm{O}}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{-5}{6} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} =\frac{5}{6} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}

options.text: {tex}\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} =6 \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}

correct_option_text: {tex}\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} =\frac{5}{6} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}

question_text: Rate law for the reaction A + 2B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C is found to be Rate = k [A][B] Concentration of reactant ‘B’ is doubled, keeping the concentration of ‘A’ constant, the value of rate constant will be ________.

Concentration of reactant ‘B’ is doubled, keeping the concentration of ‘A’ constant, the value of rate constant will be ________.

options.text: the same
options.explanation: The rate constant of a reaction does not depend upon concentrations of the reactants thus it will remain the same.

options.text: doubled
quadrupled
halved

correct_option_text: the same

subject: Chemistry

question_text: Which of the following statements is incorrect about the collison theory of chemical reaction?
question_html: Which of the following statements is incorrect about the collison theory of chemical reaction?

options.text: It considers reacting molecules or atoms to be hard spheres and ignores their structural features.
options.html: It considers reacting molecules or atoms to be hard spheres and ignores their structural features.

options.text: Number of effective collisions determines the rate of reaction.
options.html: Number of effective collisions determines the rate of reaction.

options.text: Collision of atoms or molecules possessing sufficient threshold energy results into the product formation.
options.html: Collision of atoms or molecules possessing sufficient threshold energy results into the product formation.
options.explanation: According to the postulates of collision theory, there are the following necessary conditions for any reaction to be occurring The molecule should collide with sufficient Kinetic energy called threshold energy. Their orientation must be proper so as to facilitate breaking of the bonds. The collision must be effective.

options.text: Molecules should collide with sufficient threshold energy and proper orientation for the collision to be effective.
options.html: Molecules should collide with sufficient threshold energy and proper orientation for the collision to be effective.

correct_option_text: Collision of atoms or molecules possessing sufficient threshold energy results into the product formation.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Compounds A and B react according to the following chemical equation. A (g) + 2 B (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2C (g) The concentration of either A or B was changed keeping the concentrations of one of the reactants constant and rates were measured as a function of initial concentration. The following results were obtained. Choose the correct option for the rate equations for this reaction. Experiment Initial concentration of [A]/mol L –1 Initial concentration of [B]/mol L –1 Initial rate of concentration formation of [C]/mol L –1 1. 0.30 0.30 0.10 2. 0.30 0.60 0.40 3. 0.60 0.30 0.20
question_html:
Compounds A and B react according to the following chemical equation.
A (g) + 2 B (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2C (g)
The concentration of either A or B was changed keeping the concentrations of one of the reactants constant and rates were measured as a function of initial concentration. The following results were obtained. Choose the correct option for the rate equations for this reaction.

Experiment Initial concentration of [A]/mol L–1 Initial concentration of [B]/mol L–1 Initial rate of concentration formation of [C]/mol L–1
1. 0.30 0.30 0.10
2. 0.30 0.60 0.40
3. 0.60 0.30 0.20

options.text: Rate = k [A] 2 [B]
Rate = k [A] [B] 2
options.explanation: Rate = K[A] x [B] y When the concentration of B is doubled keeping the concentration of A constant, the rate of formation of C increases by a factor of four. This indicates that the rate of reactions depends upon the square of the concentration of B. When the concentration of A is doubled, the rate of formation of C also doubles from the initial value of concentration. This shows that the rate depends on the first power of concentration of A. Hence Rate = K [A] [B] 2 .

options.text: Rate = k [A] [B]
Rate = k [A] 2 [B] 0

correct_option_text: Rate = k [A] [B] 2

question_text: Which of the following statement is not correct for the catalyst?
question_html: Which of the following statement is not correct for the catalyst?

options.text: It catalyses the forward and backward reaction to the same extent.
options.html: It catalyses the forward and backward reaction to the same extent.

options.text: It alters ∆G of the reaction.
options.explanation: A catalyst increases the rate of reaction by decreasing the activation energy of the reaction. it catalyzes the spontaneous reaction. It does not affect the Gibbs free energy {tex}\Delta G{/tex} of the reaction. Thus, the statement given in option B is incorrect.

options.text: It is a substance that does not change the equilibrium constant of a reaction.
options.html: It is a substance that does not change the equilibrium constant of a reaction.

options.text: It provides an alternate mechanism by reducing activation energy between reactants and products.
options.html: It provides an alternate mechanism by reducing activation energy between reactants and products.

correct_option_text: It alters ∆G of the reaction.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Rate law cannot be determined from the balanced chemical equation if ________. the reverse reaction is involved. it is an elementary reaction. it is a sequence of elementary reactions. any of the reactants is in excess.
question_html:
Rate law cannot be determined from the balanced chemical equation if ________.

  1. the reverse reaction is involved.
  2. it is an elementary reaction.
  3. it is a sequence of elementary reactions.
  4. any of the reactants is in excess.
solution_text: (a, c, d) are an incorrect options. Rate law can be determined from a balanced chemical equation if it is an elementary reaction.
solution_html:
(a, c, d) are an incorrect options.

Rate law can be determined from a balanced chemical equation if it is an elementary reaction.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following statements are applicable to a balanced chemical equation of an elementary reaction? Order is the same as molecularity. Order is less than the molecularity. Order is greater than the molecularity. Molecularity can never be zero.
question_html:
Which of the following statements are applicable to a balanced chemical equation of an elementary reaction?

  1. Order is the same as molecularity.
  2. Order is less than the molecularity.
  3. Order is greater than the molecularity.
  4. Molecularity can never be zero.
solution_text: (a, d) For an elementary reaction, the order and molecularity are the same. Molecularity of a reaction can never be zero or a non-integer.
solution_html: (a, d) For an elementary reaction, the order and molecularity are the same. Molecularity of a reaction can never be zero or a non-integer.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: In any unimolecular reaction ________. only one reacting species is involved in the rate-determining step. the order and the molecularity of the slowest step are equal to one. the molecularity of the reaction is one and the order is zero. both molecularity and order of the reaction are one.
question_html:
In any unimolecular reaction ________.

  1. only one reacting species is involved in the rate-determining step.
  2. the order and the molecularity of the slowest step are equal to one.
  3. the molecularity of the reaction is one and the order is zero.
  4. both molecularity and order of the reaction are one.
solution_text: (a, b) For any unimolecular reaction, only one reactant is involved in rate-determining step and the order and molecularity of rate-determining or slowest steps are equal to one. A {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} B Rate = k[A]
solution_html:
(a, b) For any unimolecular reaction, only one reactant is involved in rate-determining step and the order and molecularity of rate-determining or slowest steps are equal to one.
A {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} B
Rate = k[A]

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: For a complex reaction ________. order of the overall reaction is the same as the molecularity of the slowest step. order of the overall reaction is less than the molecularity of the slowest step. order of the overall reaction is greater than the molecularity of the slowest step. the molecularity of the slowest step is never zero or non-integer.
question_html:
For a complex reaction ________.

  1. order of the overall reaction is the same as the molecularity of the slowest step.
  2. order of the overall reaction is less than the molecularity of the slowest step.
  3. order of the overall reaction is greater than the molecularity of the slowest step.
  4. the molecularity of the slowest step is never zero or non-integer.
solution_text: (a, d) are the correct option For a complex reaction, order of overall reaction = molecularity of slowest step. As the rate of overall reaction depends upon the total number of molecules involved in the slowest step of the reaction. Hence, the molecularity of the slowest step is equal to the order of the overall reaction. Since the completion of any chemical reaction is not possible in the absence of reactants. Hence, the slowest step of any chemical reaction must contain at least one reactant. Thus, the molecularity of the slowest step is never zero or non-integer. hence, (b) and (c) is incorrect option.
solution_html:
(a, d) are the correct option
For a complex reaction, order of overall reaction = molecularity of slowest step.
As the rate of overall reaction depends upon the total number of molecules involved in the slowest step of the reaction. Hence, the molecularity of the slowest step is equal to the order of the overall reaction.
Since the completion of any chemical reaction is not possible in the absence of reactants.
Hence, the slowest step of any chemical reaction must contain at least one reactant. Thus, the molecularity of the slowest step is never zero or non-integer. hence, (b) and (c) is incorrect option.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: At high pressure the following reaction is zero order. 2NH 3 (g) {tex}\xrightarrow[{Platinum\;catalyst}]{1130\;K}{/tex} N 2 (g) + 3H 2 (g) Which of the following options are correct for this reaction? Rate of reaction = Rate constant Rate of the reaction depends on the concentration of ammonia. Rate of decomposition of ammonia will remain constant until ammonia disappears completely. Further increase in pressure will change the rate of reaction.
question_html:
At high pressure the following reaction is zero order.
2NH3 (g) {tex}\xrightarrow[{Platinum\;catalyst}]{1130\;K}{/tex} N2(g) + 3H2(g)
Which of the following options are correct for this reaction?

  1. Rate of reaction = Rate constant
  2. Rate of the reaction depends on the concentration of ammonia.
  3. Rate of decomposition of ammonia will remain constant until ammonia disappears completely.
  4. Further increase in pressure will change the rate of reaction.
solution_text: (i, iii, iv) For a zero-order reaction, the rate of reaction is independent of the concentration of ammonia. As for the zero-order reaction, the rate of reaction is equal to zero power of concentration.
solution_html: (i, iii, iv) For a zero-order reaction, the rate of reaction is independent of the concentration of ammonia. As for the zero-order reaction, the rate of reaction is equal to zero power of concentration.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: During decomposition of an activated complex energy is always released energy is always absorbed energy does not change reactants may be formed
question_html:
During decomposition of an activated complex

  1. energy is always released
  2. energy is always absorbed
  3. energy does not change
  4. reactants may be formed
solution_text: (i, iv) The activated complex is formed at the highest level of energy of the system. It is unstable and decomposes to give products with the release of energy so the final enthalpy of reactant depends upon the nature of the reactant and product. Reactants may be formed if the reaction is reversible.
solution_html: (i, iv) The activated complex is formed at the highest level of energy of the system. It is unstable and decomposes to give products with the release of energy so the final enthalpy of reactant depends upon the nature of the reactant and product. Reactants may be formed if the reaction is reversible.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: According to Maxwell Boltzmann distribution of energy, ________. the fraction of molecules with most probable kinetic energy decreases at higher temperatures. the fraction of molecules with most probable kinetic energy increases at higher temperatures. most probable kinetic energy increases at higher temperatures. most probable kinetic energy decreases at higher temperatures.
question_html:
According to Maxwell Boltzmann distribution of energy, ________.

  1. the fraction of molecules with most probable kinetic energy decreases at higher temperatures.
  2. the fraction of molecules with most probable kinetic energy increases at higher temperatures.
  3. most probable kinetic energy increases at higher temperatures.
  4. most probable kinetic energy decreases at higher temperatures.
solution_text: (i, iii) Distribution of kinetic energy may be described by plotting a graph of the fraction of molecules (N E /N T ) versus kinetic energy(E). The kinetic energy of the maximum fraction of a molecule is known as the most probable kinetic energy which corresponds to the peak of the curve. It is important to note that with an increase in temperature, the peak shifts forward but downward. This means that with an increase of temperature most probable kinetic energy increases. the fractions of molecules possessing most probable kinetic energy decreases.
solution_html:
(i, iii) Distribution of kinetic energy may be described by plotting a graph of the fraction of molecules (NE/NT) versus kinetic energy(E).

The kinetic energy of the maximum fraction of a molecule is known as the most probable kinetic energy which corresponds to the peak of the curve. It is important to note that with an increase in temperature, the peak shifts forward but downward. This means that with an increase of temperature

  1. most probable kinetic energy increases.
  2. the fractions of molecules possessing most probable kinetic energy decreases.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: In the graph showing Maxwell Boltzman distribution of energy, ________. area under the curve must not change with an increase in temperature. area under the curve increases with an increase in temperature. area under the curve decreases with an increase in temperature. with increase in the temperature, curve broadens and shifts to the right-hand side.
question_html:
In the graph showing Maxwell Boltzman distribution of energy, ________.

  1. area under the curve must not change with an increase in temperature.
  2. area under the curve increases with an increase in temperature.
  3. area under the curve decreases with an increase in temperature.
  4. with increase in the temperature, curve broadens and shifts to the right-hand side.
solution_text: (i, iv) The area under the curve remains the same with an increase in temperature and when there is an increase in temperature curve broadens and shifts to the right-hand side.
solution_html: (i, iv) The area under the curve remains the same with an increase in temperature and when there is an increase in temperature curve broadens and shifts to the right-hand side.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following statements are in accordance with the Arrhenius equation? Rate of a reaction increases with increase in temperature. Rate of a reaction increases with decrease in activation energy. Rate constant decreases exponentially with increase in temperature. Rate of reaction decreases with decrease in activation energy.
question_html:
Which of the following statements are in accordance with the Arrhenius equation?

  1. Rate of a reaction increases with increase in temperature.
  2. Rate of a reaction increases with decrease in activation energy.
  3. Rate constant decreases exponentially with increase in temperature.
  4. Rate of reaction decreases with decrease in activation energy.
solution_text: (i, ii) The rate of reaction increases with temperature. It becomes double with every {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex} change in temperature. Rate of reaction increases with a decrease in activation energy.
solution_html:
(i, ii)
The rate of reaction increases with temperature. It becomes double with every {tex}10^{\circ}{/tex} change in temperature. Rate of reaction increases with a decrease in activation energy.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Mark the incorrect statements. Catalyst provides an alternative pathway to the reaction mechanism. Catalyst raises the activation energy. Catalyst lowers the activation energy. Catalyst alters enthalpy change of the reaction.
question_html:
Mark the incorrect statements.

  1. Catalyst provides an alternative pathway to the reaction mechanism.
  2. Catalyst raises the activation energy.
  3. Catalyst lowers the activation energy.
  4. Catalyst alters enthalpy change of the reaction.
solution_text: (ii, iv) are incorrect As the catalyst is added to the reaction medium rate of reaction increases by decreasing the activation energy of the molecule. Hence, it follows an alternative pathway. Catalyst does not change the enthalpy change of the reaction. The energy of the ' reactant and product remains the same in both catalysed and uncatalysed reaction. Hence, (i) and (iii) are correct statements.
solution_html:
(ii, iv) are incorrect

  1. As the catalyst is added to the reaction medium rate of reaction increases by decreasing the activation energy of the molecule. Hence, it follows an alternative pathway.
  2. Catalyst does not change the enthalpy change of the reaction. The energy of the ' reactant and product remains the same in both catalysed and uncatalysed reaction. Hence, (i) and (iii) are correct statements.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following graphs is not correct for a zero-order reaction?
question_html: Which of the following graphs is not correct for a zero-order reaction?
solution_text: (i, iv) are not correct for zero-order reaction. If we plot [R] against t we get a straight line with slope = -k and intercept equal to[R] 0 from the equation [R] = -kt + [R] 0 For the zero-order reaction, the rate is proportional to the zero power of concentration Rate = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = k[R] 0
solution_html:
(i, iv) are not correct for zero-order reaction. If we plot [R] against t we get a straight line with slope = -k and intercept equal to[R]0 from the equation
[R] = -kt + [R]0
For the zero-order reaction, the rate is proportional to the zero power of concentration
Rate = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = k[R]0

book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following graphs is correct for a zero order reaction:
question_html: Which of the following graphs is correct for a zero order reaction:
solution_text: (i, iv) are correct for zero order reaction t 1/2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k} {/tex} and Rate = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = k[R] or log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{R}]}=\frac{k t}{2.303}{/tex} If we plot a graph between log [R] 0 /[R] versus t, the slope = {tex}\frac{k}{2.303}{/tex} .
solution_html:

t1/2 = {tex}\frac{0.693}{k} {/tex} and Rate = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = k[R]

If we plot a graph between log [R]0/[R] versus t, the slope = {tex}\frac{k}{2.303}{/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: State a condition under which a bimolecular reaction is kinetically first-order reaction.
question_html: State a condition under which a bimolecular reaction is kinetically first-order reaction.
solution_text: Let us consider a biomolecular reaction: A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Product Rate = k[A][B] ...(i) When the concentration of [B] is taken in large excess that its concentration hardly change, rate law will become Rate =k'[A] where, k' = k[B] The order of reaction will be equal to one.
solution_html:
Let us consider a biomolecular reaction:
A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Product
Rate = k[A][B] ...(i)
When the concentration of [B] is taken in large excess that its concentration hardly change, rate law will become Rate =k'[A]
where, k' = k[B]
The order of reaction will be equal to one.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Write the rate equation for the reaction 2A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C if the order of the reaction is zero.
question_html: Write the rate equation for the reaction 2A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C if the order of the reaction is zero.
solution_text: 2A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C if the rate of reaction is zero it can be represented as Rate = {tex}\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\circ}[\mathrm{B}]^{\circ}{/tex} = k
solution_html:
2A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C

if the rate of reaction is zero it can be represented as
Rate = {tex}\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\circ}[\mathrm{B}]^{\circ}{/tex} = k

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: How can you determine the rate law of the following reaction? 2NO (g) + O 2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NO 2 (g)
question_html:
How can you determine the rate law of the following reaction?
2NO (g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2NO2 (g)
solution_text: The rate law can be determined by measuring the rate of this reaction as a function of initial concentration by keeping the concentration of one of the reactants constant and changing the concentration of other reactants or by changing the concentration of both the reactants. From the concentration dependence of rate, rate law can be determined. The rate law for any reaction cannot be predicted by merely looking at balanced chemical equation.
solution_html: The rate law can be determined by measuring the rate of this reaction as a function of initial concentration by keeping the concentration of one of the reactants constant and changing the concentration of other reactants or by changing the concentration of both the reactants. From the concentration dependence of rate, rate law can be determined. The rate law for any reaction cannot be predicted by merely looking at balanced chemical equation.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: For which type of reactions, order and molecularity have the same value?
question_html: For which type of reactions, order and molecularity have the same value?
solution_text: Elementary reactions have the same value of order and molecularity because the elementary reaction proceeds in a single step.
solution_html: Elementary reactions have the same value of order and molecularity because the elementary reaction proceeds in a single step.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: In a reaction, if the concentration of reactant A is tripled, the rate of reaction becomes twenty-seven times. What is the order of the reaction?
question_html: In a reaction, if the concentration of reactant A is tripled, the rate of reaction becomes twenty-seven times. What is the order of the reaction?
solution_text: Rate = {tex}k[A]^{\alpha}{/tex} (i) 27 {tex}\times{/tex} Rate = {tex}k[3A]^{\alpha}{/tex} (ii) Dividing eq. (ii) by eq. (i), we get (3) {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 27 = (3) 3 thus, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Order of reaction = 3
solution_html:
Rate = {tex}k[A]^{\alpha}{/tex} (i)
27 {tex}\times{/tex} Rate = {tex}k[3A]^{\alpha}{/tex} (ii)
Dividing eq. (ii) by eq. (i), we get (3){tex}\alpha{/tex} = 27 = (3)3 thus, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Order of reaction = 3

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Derive an expression to calculate time required for completion of zero order reaction.
question_html: Derive an expression to calculate time required for completion of zero order reaction.
solution_text: For a zero order reaction, R = [R] 0 - kt For completion of the reaction [R] = 0 ∴ kt = [R] 0 Or {tex}t=\frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
For a zero order reaction,
R = [R]0 - kt
For completion of the reaction [R] = 0
∴ kt = [R]0
Or {tex}t=\frac{[\mathrm{R}]_{0}}{k}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: For a reaction A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Products, the rate law is - Rate = k [A][B] 3/2 Can the reaction be an elementary reaction? Explain.
question_html: For a reaction A + B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Products, the rate law is - Rate = k [A][B]3/2 Can the reaction be an elementary reaction? Explain.
solution_text: An elementary reaction is a chemical reaction in which one or more chemical species react directly to form a product. Rate = k[A][B] 3/2 Order = {tex}1+\frac{3}{2}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} The order of elementary reaction cannot be fractional.
solution_html:
An elementary reaction is a chemical reaction in which one or more chemical species react directly to form a product.

Rate = k[A][B]3/2
Order = {tex}1+\frac{3}{2}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
The order of elementary reaction cannot be fractional.

book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: For a certain reaction large fraction of molecules has energy more than the threshold energy, yet the rate of reaction is very slow. Why?
question_html: For a certain reaction large fraction of molecules has energy more than the threshold energy, yet the rate of reaction is very slow. Why?
solution_text: Though the reacting large fraction of molecules may be having energy more than threshold energy, yet they may not be effective due to lack of proper orientation.
solution_html: Though the reacting large fraction of molecules may be having energy more than threshold energy, yet they may not be effective due to lack of proper orientation.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: For a zero order reaction will the molecularity be equal to zero? Explain.
question_html: For a zero order reaction will the molecularity be equal to zero? Explain.
solution_text: Molecularity of a reaction can never be equal to zero as it shows the number of reactants taking part in a reaction, which can never be zero.
solution_html: Molecularity of a reaction can never be equal to zero as it shows the number of reactants taking part in a reaction, which can never be zero.

question_text: For a general reaction A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B, plot of concentration of A vs time is given in Fig. Answer the following question on the basis of this graph. What is the order of the reaction? What is the slope of the curve? What are the units of rate constant?
question_html:

  1. What is the order of the reaction?
  2. What is the slope of the curve?
  3. What are the units of rate constant?
solution_text: Order=Zero order reaction [R] = [R] 0 - kt {tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope = -k {tex}\frac{\mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{s}^{-1}}{/tex} = k[A] 0 or k = mol -1 L s -1
solution_html:
1. Order=Zero order reaction
  2. [R] = [R]0 - kt
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope = -k
  3. {tex}\frac{\mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{s}^{-1}}{/tex} = k[A]0 or k = mol-1 L s-1

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The reaction between H 2 (g) and O 2 (g) is highly feasible yet allowing the gases to stand at room temperature in the same vessel does not lead to the formation of water. Explain
question_html: The reaction between H2(g) and O2(g) is highly feasible yet allowing the gases to stand at room temperature in the same vessel does not lead to the formation of water. Explain
solution_text: 2 H 2 (g) + O 2 (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2 H 2 O(l) This reaction does not take place at the room temperature because the activation energy of the reaction is very high.
solution_html:
2 H2(g) + O2(g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2 H2O(l)
This reaction does not take place at the room temperature because the activation energy of the reaction is very high.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why does the rate of a reaction increase with rise in temperature?
question_html: Why does the rate of a reaction increase with rise in temperature?
solution_text: At higher temperatures, a larger fraction of colliding particles can cross the energy barrier (i.e. the activation energy E ​​​​​​a ), which leads to a faster rate of a reaction.
solution_html: At higher temperatures, a larger fraction of colliding particles can cross the energy barrier (i.e. the activation energy E​​​​​​a), which leads to a faster rate of a reaction.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Oxygen is available in plenty in air yet fuels do not burn by themselves at room temperature. Explain.
question_html: Oxygen is available in plenty in air yet fuels do not burn by themselves at room temperature. Explain.
solution_text: The activation energy for combustion reactions of fuels is very high at room temperature, therefore, they do not burn by themselves at room temperature.
solution_html: The activation energy for combustion reactions of fuels is very high at room temperature, therefore, they do not burn by themselves at room temperature.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why is the probability of reaction with molecularity higher than three very rare?
question_html: Why is the probability of reaction with molecularity higher than three very rare?
solution_text: As the number increases the chance of simultaneous collision decrease that means the probability of more than three molecules colliding simultaneously is very small. Therefore, the possibility of molecularity being three is very low.
solution_html: As the number increases the chance of simultaneous collision decrease that means the probability of more than three molecules colliding simultaneously is very small. Therefore, the possibility of molecularity being three is very low.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why does the rate of any reaction generally decreases during the course of the reaction?
question_html: Why does the rate of any reaction generally decreases during the course of the reaction?
solution_text: The rate of a reaction depends on the concentration of reactants. As the rate of a reaction progresses, the concentration of reactants decreases because the reactant's concentration starts getting converted to products. Hence the rate of a reaction decreases.
solution_html: The rate of a reaction depends on the concentration of reactants. As the rate of a reaction progresses, the concentration of reactants decreases because the reactant's concentration starts getting converted to products. Hence the rate of a reaction decreases.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Thermodynamic feasibility of the reaction alone cannot decide the rate of the reaction. Explain with the help of one example.
question_html: Thermodynamic feasibility of the reaction alone cannot decide the rate of the reaction. Explain with the help of one example.
solution_text: Thermodynamic feasibility of a reaction depends on Gibbs free energy i.e., {tex}\Delta{/tex} G must be negative for a spontaneous process. Kinetic feasibility depends on the activation energy of reaction, the lesser is the activation energy, the greater is the feasibility of reaction, i.e. i.e., Diamond {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Graphite {tex}\Delta{/tex} G = -ve This process is thermodynamically feasible but it is very slow due to its high activation energy Hence, thermodynamic feasibility of the reaction alone cannot decide the rate of reaction.
solution_html:
Thermodynamic feasibility of a reaction depends on Gibbs free energy i.e.,  {tex}\Delta{/tex}G must be negative for a spontaneous process. Kinetic feasibility depends on the activation energy of reaction, the lesser is the activation energy, the greater is the feasibility of reaction, i.e.
i.e., Diamond {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Graphite {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -ve
This process is thermodynamically feasible but it is very slow due to its high activation energy Hence, thermodynamic feasibility of the reaction alone cannot decide the rate of reaction.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why in the redox titration of KMnO 4 versus oxalic acid do we heat oxalic acid solution before starting the titration?
question_html: Why in the redox titration of KMnO4 versus oxalic acid do we heat oxalic acid solution before starting the titration?
solution_text: The reaction in redox titration between KMnO 4 and oxalic acid is very slow. By raising the temperature we can increase the rate of reaction.
solution_html: The reaction in redox titration between KMnO4 and oxalic acid is very slow. By raising the temperature we can increase the rate of reaction.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why can’t the molecularity of any reaction be equal to zero?
question_html: Why can’t the molecularity of any reaction be equal to zero?
solution_text: Molecularity of a reaction means the number of molecules of the reactants taking place in an elementary reaction. Since at least one molecule must be present, so that molecularity will be at least one.
solution_html: Molecularity of a reaction means the number of molecules of the reactants taking place in an elementary reaction. Since at least one molecule must be present, so that molecularity will be at least one.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why is molecularity applicable only for elementary reactions and order applicable for elementary as well as complex reaction?
question_html: Why is molecularity applicable only for elementary reactions and order applicable for elementary as well as complex reaction?
solution_text: A complex reaction occurs through several elementary reactions gives us products. The numbers of molecules involved in each elementary reaction may be different i.e., the molecularity (reaction taking place in one step) of the step may be different. Therefore, the molecularity of the overall complex reaction is meaningless. On the other hand, the order of a complex reaction is experimentally determined by the slowest step in its mechanism and is, therefore, applicable even in the case of complex reactions.
solution_html: A complex reaction occurs through several elementary reactions gives us products. The numbers of molecules involved in each elementary reaction may be different i.e., the molecularity (reaction taking place in one step) of the step may be different. Therefore, the molecularity of the overall complex reaction is meaningless. On the other hand, the order of a complex reaction is experimentally determined by the slowest step in its mechanism and is, therefore, applicable even in the case of complex reactions.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why can we not determine the order of a reaction by taking into consideration the balanced chemical equation?
question_html: Why can we not determine the order of a reaction by taking into consideration the balanced chemical equation?
solution_text: Balanced chemical equation often leads to an incorrect order or rate law. For example, the following reaction appears to be a tenth order reaction. KClO 3 + 6FeSO 4 + 3H 2 SO 4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} KCl + 3H 2 O + 3Fe 2 (SO 4 ) 3 However, this is actually a second-order reaction (chemical reaction in which sum of exponents in the rate law is equal to 2) Actually, the reaction is complex and occurs in several steps. The order of such reaction is determined by the slowest step in the reaction mechanism. Order is determined experimentally and gives the actual dependence of the observed rate of a reaction on the concentration of reactants.
solution_html:
Balanced chemical equation often leads to an incorrect order or rate law. For example, the following reaction appears to be a tenth order reaction.
KClO3 + 6FeSO4 + 3H2SO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} KCl + 3H2O + 3Fe2(SO4)3
However, this is actually a second-order reaction (chemical reaction in which sum of exponents in the rate law is equal to 2) Actually, the reaction is complex and occurs in several steps. The order of such reaction is determined by the slowest step in the reaction mechanism. Order is determined experimentally and gives the actual dependence of the observed rate of a reaction on the concentration of reactants.

(c)

question_text: Match the statements given in column I and Column II Column I Column II (a) Catalyst alters the rate of reaction (i) can not be fraction or zero (b) Molecularity (ii) proper orientation is not there always (c) Second half- life of first-order reaction (iii) by lowering the activation energy (d) {tex}e^\frac{-E_a}{RT}{/tex} (iv) is same as the first (e) Energetically favourable reactions are sometimes slow (v) total probability is one (f) Area under the Maxwell Boltzmann curve is constant (vi) refers to the fraction of molecules with energy equal to or greater than activation energy.
question_html:
Match the statements given in column I and Column II

Column I  Column II
(a) Catalyst alters the rate of reaction  (i) can not be fraction or zero 
(b) Molecularity  (ii) proper orientation is not there always
(c) Second half- life of first-order reaction  (iii) by lowering the activation energy 
(d) {tex}e^\frac{-E_a}{RT}{/tex} (iv) is same as the first 
(e) Energetically favourable reactions are sometimes slow (v) total probability is one 
(f) Area under the Maxwell Boltzmann curve is constant 

(vi) refers to the fraction of molecules with energy equal to or greater than activation energy.
solution_text: The correct order of match is (a)-(ii), (b)-(v), (c)-(i), (d)-(iii), (e)-(iv), (f)-(iv).
solution_html: The correct order of match is (a)-(ii), (b)-(v), (c)-(i), (d)-(iii), (e)-(iv), (f)-(iv).

Reason (R): Order is determined experimentally and molecularity is the sum of the stoichiometric coefficient of the rate-determining elementary step.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Assertion (A): The enthalpy of reaction remains constant in the presence of a catalyst. Reason (R): A catalyst participating in the reaction, forms a different activated complex and lowers down the activation energy but the difference in energy of reactant and product remains the same.
question_html:
Assertion (A): The enthalpy of reaction remains constant in the presence of a catalyst.
Reason (R): A catalyst participating in the reaction, forms a different activated complex and lowers down the activation energy but the difference in energy of reactant and product remains the same.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.explanation: AH = Activation Energy of forwarding reaction - Activation Energy of the reverse reaction. Catalyst does not alter heat of reaction because it affects the activation energy of forward and reverse reactions equally.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

correct_option_text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Assertion (A): All collisions of reactant molecules lead to product formation. Reason (R): Only those collisions in which molecules have correct orientation and sufficient kinetic energy lead to the compound formation.
question_html:
Assertion (A): All collisions of reactant molecules lead to product formation.
Reason (R): Only those collisions in which molecules have correct orientation and sufficient kinetic energy lead to the compound formation.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.
options.explanation: Every collision among reactant molecules does not lead to the formation of the product. Only effective collision brings out the formation of the product.

correct_option_text: A is false but R is true.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Assertion (A): Rate constants determined from the Arrhenius equation are fairly accurate for simple as well as complex molecules. Reason (R): Reactant molecules undergo chemical change irrespective of their orientation during the collision.
question_html:
Assertion (A): Rate constants determined from the Arrhenius equation are fairly accurate for simple as well as complex molecules.
Reason (R): Reactant molecules undergo chemical change irrespective of their orientation during the collision.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.html: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.
options.explanation: According to the Arrhenius equation (k = Ae -Ea/RT ); it is found almost accurate for single as well as complex reactions. However, orientation is essential for the reactant molecules participating in the reaction.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
options.html: A is false but R is true.

correct_option_text: A is true but R is false.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: All energetically effective collisions do not result in a chemical change. Explain with the help of an example.
question_html: All energetically effective collisions do not result in a chemical change. Explain with the help of an example.
solution_text: Only effective collision leads to the formation of products. It means that collisions in which molecules collide with sufficient kinetic energy (called threshold energy = activation energy + energy possessed by reacting species). And proper orientation leads to a chemical change (this, forming products)it facilitates the breaking of old bonds between (reactant) molecules and the formation of the new ones, i.e., in products. e.g. formation of methanol from bromomethane depends upon the orientation of the reactant molecules. The proper orientation of reactant molecules leads to bond formation whereas improper orientation makes them simply back and no products are formed. To account for effective collisions, another factor P (probability or steric factor) is introduced K = Ae -Ea/RT (Arrhenius equation).
solution_html:
Only effective collision leads to the formation of products. It means that collisions in which molecules collide with sufficient kinetic energy (called threshold energy = activation energy + energy possessed by reacting species). And proper orientation leads to a chemical change (this, forming products)it facilitates the breaking of old bonds between (reactant) molecules and the formation of the new ones, i.e., in products.
e.g. formation of methanol from bromomethane depends upon the orientation of the reactant molecules.

The proper orientation of reactant molecules leads to bond formation whereas improper orientation makes them simply back and no products are formed. To account for effective collisions, another factor P (probability or steric factor) is introduced K = Ae-Ea/RT(Arrhenius equation).

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: What happens to most probable kinetic energy and the energy of activation with an increase in temperature?
question_html: What happens to most probable kinetic energy and the energy of activation with an increase in temperature?
solution_text: When the temperature has raised the maximum of the curve moves to the higher energy value and the curve broadens out i.e spread to the right so that there is a greater proportion of the molecules with much higher energies area under the curve must constant. Most probable kinetic energy increases with an increase in temperature. As the temperature increases, activation energy E a decreases which will result in an increase in the rate of reaction.
solution_html: When the temperature has raised the maximum of the curve moves to the higher energy value and the curve broadens out i.e spread to the right so that there is a greater proportion of the molecules with much higher energies area under the curve must constant. Most probable kinetic energy increases with an increase in temperature. As the temperature increases, activation energy Ea decreases which will result in an increase in the rate of reaction.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Describe how does the enthalpy of reaction remains unchanged when a catalyst is used in the reaction.
question_html: Describe how does the enthalpy of reaction remains unchanged when a catalyst is used in the reaction.
solution_text: A catalyst is a substance that increases the speed of a reaction without itself undergoing any permanent chemical change. According to "intermediate complex formation theory" reactants first, combined with the catalyst to form an intermediate complex that is short-lived and decomposes to form the products and regenerating the catalyst. The intermediate formed has much lower potential energy than the intermediate complex formed between the reactants in the absence of the catalyst. Thus, the presence of catalyst lowers the potential energy barrier and the reaction follows a new alternative pathway or reaction mechanism which requires less activation energy of reactants and product. We know that the lower the activation energy, the faster is the rate of a reaction because more reactant molecules can cross the energy barrier and change into products. Enthalpy, AH is a state function. Enthalpy of reaction, i.e., the difference in energy between reactants and product is constant, which is clear from the potential energy diagram. Potential energy diagram of catalyzed reaction is given as
solution_html:
A catalyst is a substance that increases the speed of a reaction without itself undergoing any permanent chemical change.
According to "intermediate complex formation theory" reactants first, combined with the catalyst to form an intermediate complex that is short-lived and decomposes to form the products and regenerating the catalyst.
The intermediate formed has much lower potential energy than the intermediate complex formed between the reactants in the absence of the catalyst.
Thus, the presence of catalyst lowers the potential energy barrier and the reaction follows a new alternative pathway or reaction mechanism which requires less activation energy of reactants and product. We know that the lower the activation energy, the faster is the rate of a reaction because more reactant molecules can cross the energy barrier and change into products. Enthalpy, AH is a state function. Enthalpy of reaction, i.e., the difference in energy between reactants and product is constant, which is clear from the potential energy diagram.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Explain the difference between the instantaneous rate of a reaction and the average rate of a reaction.
question_html: Explain the difference between the instantaneous rate of a reaction and the average rate of a reaction.
solution_text: Average rate (R av ) Instantaneous rate(R av ) i. Average rate of reaction is the change in the concentration of reactant or product in a specified time interval. i. Instantaneous rate of reaction is the change of concentration of reactant or product at particular moment of time. ii. It is concerned with the whole reaction process. ii. It is concerned with the particular moment of reaction. iii. Average rat = {tex}-\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{R}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{P}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} iii. Instantaneous rate = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=+\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{P}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Average rate (Rav) Instantaneous rate(Rav)
i. Average rate of reaction is the change in the concentration of reactant or product in a specified time interval. i. Instantaneous rate of reaction is the change of concentration of reactant or product at particular moment of time.
ii. It is concerned with the whole reaction process. ii. It is concerned with the particular moment of reaction.
iii. Average rat = {tex}-\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{R}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=+\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{P}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} iii. Instantaneous rate = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{R}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=+\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{P}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: With the help of an example explain what is meant by the pseudo-first-order reaction.
question_html: With the help of an example explain what is meant by the pseudo-first-order reaction.
solution_text: A reaction in which one reactant is present in large amounts and its concentration does not get altered during the course of the reaction, behaves as the first-order reaction. Such a reaction is called a pseudo-first-order reaction. For example, (i) hydrolysis of 0.01 mol of ethyl acetate with 10 mol of water, the amount of various constitute at the beginning (t=0) and completion (t) is given by:
